intro:
The Epistle to the Hebrews is anonymous: the text names no author and, uniquely among the New Testament letters, opens without the customary greeting that identifies a writer. Its authorship has been debated since the earliest centuries of the church, and no tradition ever settled the question with certainty.

The Eastern church tended to associate the letter with the apostle Paul, which secured its early acceptance in the East; the Western church long hesitated to receive it precisely because its Pauline authorship was doubted. Origen of Alexandria summed up the matter in a verdict often quoted ever since: the thoughts may be the apostle's, but the polished Greek and arrangement are another's, and “who wrote the epistle, in truth God knows.”

sections.title: Traditional attributions
sections.body: Across history the letter has been ascribed to several figures: Paul (the dominant Eastern view, and the basis of its place after Paul's letters in many Bibles); Barnabas (Tertullian's claim); Apollos, the learned Alexandrian ‘mighty in the Scriptures’ (a view favoured by Luther and many since); Luke, Clement of Rome, Silas, and even Priscilla. Each proposal rests on style or association rather than direct evidence.

sections.title: Internal evidence
sections.body: The letter's polished, rhetorical Greek differs markedly from Paul's, and its writer places himself among those who received the gospel from the first witnesses rather than from the risen Christ directly (Hebrews 2:3) — a statement difficult to square with Paul's insistence on his own direct apostolic call. At the same time the closing greetings mention Timothy and reflect a Pauline circle (Hebrews 13:23), which explains the persistent link to Paul.

sections.title: Why the question matters
sections.body: Because Paul's name carried apostolic authority, the authorship question affected the letter's canonicity: the West fully received Hebrews only as its inspiration was recognized apart from the name attached to it. The church's eventual judgment was that the letter's authority rests on its God-breathed content, not on identifying its human writer.
